title: Accumulating
steps.0.text: Rewrite the four cup lines with the short form. The total should be 40 — one of the lines does not add.
steps.1.text: The stock too. From 50 there should be 10 left.
steps.2.text: And the orders. They are already 2 — here only the form changes.
checkLabels.t3: The total is not typed in
checkLabels.t4: The cups use the short form
checkLabels.t6: The stock uses the short form
checkLabels.t8: The orders use the short form
why.empty: script.js is empty. You deleted everything instead of fixing it.
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: The sales are 12, 8, 15 and 5. Add them up in your head and look at what the console shows.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel on the right.
why.typed-answer: You typed the total in instead of accumulating it.
hint2.typed-answer: Tomorrow the sales will be different. The lines have to add up, not announce the result.
why.long-cups: The cups are still added the long way.
hint2.long-cups: `cups = cups + 12` can be written shorter. The plus sign goes BEFORE the equals sign.
why.long-stock: The stock is still calculated the long way.
why.long-orders: The orders are still counted the long way.
hint2.long-orders: Adding one has a sign of its own — two pluses stuck together. `orders += 1` works too.
hint2.wrong-cups: 12 plus 8 plus 15 plus 5 makes 40. Read the four lines one at a time and find the one that does not add.
hint3.wrong-cups: One of the lines does not accumulate, it replaces. Everything gathered before it disappears and the counting starts over.
hint4.wrong-cups: Look very closely at the line with 15. The two signs are the wrong way round.
hint2.wrong-stock: The stock starts at 50 and the sales are taken off it. If the sales are wrong, the remainder is wrong too.
why.wrong-count: There are not three lines in the console.
blocks.1.text: Four sales are added into a total. Then the sales are taken off the stock, and the orders are counted one at a time. The code runs and does not crash:
blocks.3.text: Twelve plus eight plus fifteen plus five makes 40. The console shows 20. One of the four lines does not add — it replaces, and everything gathered before it disappears.
blocks.5.text: Take the value, add something, put it back under the same name. This happens so often that it has its own form:
blocks.7.text: Both lines do the same thing, which is why the result is 24 and not 12. The second is not read left to right like a sentence — it is read as an action: add twelve to the cups. There are short forms for every operation: `-=`, `*=`, `/=`.
blocks.9.text: ⚠ One important thing about them: they run LAST. The right-hand side is worked out whole before being applied. That is why this line gives 16 and not 11 — three plus five makes eight first, then the two is multiplied by eight. Lesson ten was about precedence; this is the same rule applied here.
blocks.11.text: Adding one is even more common. It has a sign of its own, and you will need it in every loop from section four onwards:
blocks.13.text: One up, one down. Nothing more. `orders += 1` works too — it is the same, just longer.
blocks.15.text: ⚠ And `++` does not act on a number, it acts on a NAME. This line crashes. The reason is simple: counting assigns a new value to something, and you cannot assign anything to the number three — it is three.
blocks.16.text: That is also why `++` does not work on a `const`. You will also meet the other form, `++orders`, in front of the name. The difference shows up only if you use the returned value inside another expression — the trailing one returns the old value, the leading one the new. Do not write like that. Know it so you recognize it.
blocks.18.text: The plus sign goes BEFORE the equals sign. The other way round is still valid code — but it means something completely different:
blocks.20.text: `a =+ 5` is not an error. The computer reads it as "let a point at plus five" — an equals sign followed by a unary plus in front of the number. That is an assignment, not an accumulation. Everything gathered so far disappears and nobody complains, because the line is perfectly correct — it just does not do what you thought.
blocks.21.quote: Two signs, swapped around. That is the difference between a correct total and a silent loss.
blocks.23.text: The short form inherits everything from the sign it shortens — including the plus sign's double life from lesson seven:
blocks.25.text: With text `+=` joins rather than adds. That is useful when you really are building a string. It is dangerous when you thought you were calculating and somewhere along the way the number became text.
blocks.27.items.0: `cups += 12` is the same as `cups = cups + 12`. There are also `-=`, `*=`, `/=`.
blocks.27.items.1: Short forms run last. The right-hand side is worked out whole.
blocks.27.items.2: `++` and `--` add and subtract one. They act on a name only, not on a number.
blocks.27.items.3: `=+` is valid code that replaces instead of accumulating. The order of the two signs decides everything.
blocks.27.items.4: `+=` on text joins, exactly like the ordinary plus.
walkthrough.0.text: The four cup lines become `+=`, including the broken one. Here fixing and shortening are the same action: `cups =+ 15` and `cups += 15` differ by the position of two characters, and the result differs by twenty.
walkthrough.1.text: Notice that the first two lines were correct and we rewrite them anyway. Not because they are wrong, but because four lines written two different ways read more slowly than four identical ones. Uniformity is not decoration — it is what lets you spot the line that stands out.
walkthrough.2.text: `stock -= cups;` — the same form, a different operation. The stock starts at 50 and the accumulated total is taken off it. The 10 only comes out because the cups are now 40; while they were 20, this line gave 30 and looked correct.
walkthrough.3.text: `orders++;` twice. These two lines were CORRECT before the fix — the orders were 2 in the starter code too. Nothing is being repaired here, only the form is changing. Not every change is a fix.
walkthrough.4.text: `orders += 1;` works too — the check accepts both, because it looks at what the code does, not which sign it is written with. `++` is the shorter one and it is what you will meet in the loops from section four.
walkthrough.5.text: The number 40 appears nowhere in the code and one of the checks guards exactly that. The total is a result, not data — it is accumulated from the four sales. Tomorrow the sales will be different and the code has to work without you touching it.
